cards.front: What is the substrate type in a PMOS?
cards.back: n-type substrate. This is the opposite of NMOS (which uses p-type). P⁺ source/drain regions are embedded in this n-type material.

cards.front: What sign is the PMOS threshold voltage VT?
cards.back: Negative (typically −1 to −2 V). Turn-ON condition: VGS < VT, meaning the gate must be pulled sufficiently below the source voltage.

cards.front: How does the p-channel form in PMOS?
cards.back: Negative VGS creates a field that repels electrons and attracts holes to the surface of the n-type substrate, forming a p-type inversion layer (the channel).

cards.front: Which way does conventional current flow in PMOS?
cards.back: Source to Drain. Since holes flow S→D and they are positive carriers, conventional current follows: S→D. (Opposite of NMOS which is D→S.)

cards.front: Why is PMOS slower than NMOS?
cards.back: Hole mobility μp is about 2.5× lower than electron mobility μn. Since Kp = μpCox'(W/L), PMOS needs a wider channel (larger W) to match NMOS current/speed.

cards.front: How much wider must PMOS be to match NMOS current?
cards.back: About 2.5× wider, because μp ≈ μn/2.5. This is why PMOS transistors always appear larger in chip layouts.

cards.front: In CMOS, what does the PMOS transistor do?
cards.back: Acts as the pull-up network. Source connects to VDD (positive supply). When gate is LOW → PMOS ON → output pulled to VDD (logic HIGH).

cards.front: Write the PMOS saturation current equation.
cards.back: IDS = (Kp/2)(|VGS| − |VT|)². Same form as NMOS but using absolute values and Kp = μpCox'(W/L).

cards.front: How do you determine the PMOS operating region?
cards.back: Same as NMOS but with absolute values: (1) Is |VGS| > |VT|? No → Cutoff. (2) Is |VDS| < |VGS| − |VT|? Yes → Triode. No → Saturation.

cards.front: What was wrong with PMOS-only logic?
cards.back: Slower than NMOS logic because of lower hole mobility. It was used in the 1960s-70s but replaced by NMOS and then CMOS technology.

cards.front: Complete the duality: NMOS substrate = p-type → PMOS substrate = ?
cards.back: n-type. Every aspect of PMOS mirrors NMOS: substrate type, S/D doping, carrier type, VT sign, current direction — all reversed.

cards.front: In a CMOS inverter with VDD supply, when is the PMOS ON?
cards.back: When the input (gate) is LOW (0 V). Then VGS = 0 − VDD = −VDD. Since |−VDD| > |VT| (typically), the PMOS turns ON and pulls output to VDD.
